description: A paired animal structure adapted for flight or control in air is 'wapile'. The word covers the wings of birds, bats, and insects, though their anatomy differs sharply and some no longer sustain flight. 'philea' (feather) names one bird structure; 'wapi' (fly) is the motion.
articulatory_notes: Rounded lips shape voiced 'w' and open into 'a'. They meet for stressed 'pi'; lateral 'l' then touches the alveolar ridge and releases into front 'e'.
sound_symbolism: The first two syllables reproduce 'wapi' (fly). The added lateral ending extends the sound just enough to keep the noun and verb distinct in the ear.
usage_notes: The animal precedes the part when needed, as in 'pelori wapile' (bird wing) and 'nireku wapile' (insect wing). A count may omit a classifier because the body-part relation is already clear.

semantic_domains.nature: a paired animal structure adapted for flight or aerodynamic control
semantic_domains.physical: a body structure that produces lift or controls movement through air
